planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown stock in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial shade to full sun; sweetbay magnolia is more tolerant of wet, boggy soils than most magnolias and thrives in the heavy, seasonally wet soils common in southeastern gardens.

directions: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown stock in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial shade to full sun; sweetbay magnolia is more tolerant of wet, boggy soils than most magnolias and thrives in the heavy, seasonally wet soils common in southeastern gardens. Magnolia virginiana is the finest native magnolia for year-round SE gardens — semi-evergreen in zones 7–10 with smooth, blue-gray bark; large, glossy, dark-green leaves with silver undersides that flutter beautifully in the breeze; and creamy-white, lemon-scented flowers 2–3 inches across that open throughout summer, providing a long season of fragrance and pollinator value. The red, berry-like seeds in fall are consumed by birds, and the tree supports specialist Promethea silk moth and tiger swallowtail caterpillars; it grows naturally in swampy, streamside habitats from Massachusetts to Texas.

planting.note: Plant container or balled stock in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; common witch hazel is highly shade-tolerant and grows naturally as an understory tree in mature eastern deciduous forests.

directions: Plant container or balled stock in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; common witch hazel is highly shade-tolerant and grows naturally as an understory tree in mature eastern deciduous forests. Hamamelis virginiana is the source of the distilled witch hazel extract used in skincare, first-aid, and medicinal products worldwide, and has extraordinary ornamental value as a native shrub/small tree that blooms with wavy, spider-like, bright-yellow fragrant flowers directly on bare branches from October through December — the latest-blooming native woody plant in eastern North America. It also provides outstanding fall foliage color in shades of yellow to orange-red, is a critical late-season nectar source for witch hazel bees (Andrena astragali) and other fall-active insects, and the ballistic seed capsules that eject seeds up to 30 feet give rise to its folk name. Extremely adaptable, cold-hardy, and long-lived.

planting.note: Plant container or field-grown stock in spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spicebush is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native shrubs and grows naturally as a dense understory shrub in moist, rich, deciduous forests — it is exceptional in deeply shaded garden spots where few shrubs thrive.

directions: Plant container or field-grown stock in spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spicebush is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native shrubs and grows naturally as a dense understory shrub in moist, rich, deciduous forests — it is exceptional in deeply shaded garden spots where few shrubs thrive. Lindera benzoin is one of the most valuable native shrubs for wildlife gardens: the aromatic, spicy-scented foliage is the exclusive larval host plant for the spicebush swallowtail (Papilio troilus) and the promethea silk moth; small, bright-yellow flowers appear in late winter to early spring before the leaves, providing critical early nectar; glossy, red, waxy berries in fall are greedily consumed by migrating songbirds, especially wood thrushes and veeries; and brilliant yellow fall foliage lights up shaded woodland garden interiors. Dioecious — plant male and female for berries.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown container stock only in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; leatherwood is notoriously difficult to propagate and transplant — purchase from reputable native plant nurseries and disturb the root system as little as possible.

directions: Plant nursery-grown container stock only in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; leatherwood is notoriously difficult to propagate and transplant — purchase from reputable native plant nurseries and disturb the root system as little as possible. Dirca palustris is one of the most shade-tolerant, slow-growing, and long-lived of all native North American shrubs, persisting for decades in deep shade under mature forest canopies in moist, rich woodland soils from Newfoundland to Florida; tiny, pale-yellow, tubular flowers appear in late winter before any leaves on bare, supple branches that are so flexible and strong they cannot be broken by hand (giving rise to its common name), and small, olive-green, berry-like fruits ripen in late spring. The plant is extremely patient and will eventually form a graceful, rounded mound; it is an underused jewel for shaded native plant gardens and rain gardens in moist, acid forest soils.

common_name: Vernal Witch Hazel, Ozark Witch Hazel

planting.note: Plant container or balled stock in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; vernal witch hazel is native to the Ozark plateau and grows naturally along stream gravels — it is more tolerant of dry, calcareous soils than H. virginiana and slightly more drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant container or balled stock in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; vernal witch hazel is native to the Ozark plateau and grows naturally along stream gravels — it is more tolerant of dry, calcareous soils than H. virginiana and slightly more drought-tolerant once established. Hamamelis vernalis is among the first woody plants to bloom in late winter, producing intensely fragrant, small, yellow to reddish-orange, ribbon-petaled flowers on bare stems from January through March, filling the dormant winter garden with a spicy-sweet scent on any mild winter day; the flowers close when temperatures drop below freezing and reopen when temperatures rise, allowing them to survive late-winter freezes. The fall foliage is outstanding in shades of gold and orange; it is a major improvement over Asian witch hazels for naturalizing in eastern North American native gardens because it supports specialist witch hazel bees and other native insects.
